output:
# HUSH and TETHER: Curing the Drift-Blind, Tail-Silent Learning-Rate-Bound Blind Spot in Personalized Federated Learning with a Per-Client, Per-Layer, Exact-Collapse Dynamic Bound

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Federated learning (FL) trains a shared model over decentralized clients without centralizing their data: the server broadcasts a global model, each client trains locally, uploads its update, and the server aggregates — canonically by weighted averaging in **FedAvg** (McMahan et al., AISTATS 2017) [1]. The defining obstacle is **statistical heterogeneity**: client data are Non-IID and unbalanced (label skew, feature skew, quantity skew), so local objectives are mutually inconsistent and the single averaged global model generalizes poorly to any individual client (Zhao et al. 2018 [18]; Kairouz et al. 2021 [17]). **FedProx** (Li et al., MLSys 2020) [2] adds a proximal term `(μ/2)‖w − w^t‖²` to anchor local training to the global model and stabilize under heterogeneity; **SCAFFOLD** (Karimireddy et al., ICML 2020) [3] introduces server/client **control variates** that correct client-drift in the update direction at the cost of doubling per-round communication; **FedNova** (Wang et al., NeurIPS 2020) [12] normalizes heterogeneous local step counts to remove objective inconsistency; **FAVOR** (Wang et al., INFOCOM 2020) [19] selects clients via deep reinforcement learning and **FedMA** (Wang et al., ICLR 2020) [20] aggregates layer-wise via matched averaging. Across all of these, a **single global model still generalizes poorly to each client** — the motivation for personalization.

Personalized FL (pFL) accepts that no single model fits all clients and instead produces client-adapted models. Surveys organize the field into three families (Tan et al., IEEE TNNLS 2022 [16]). (i) **Regularization / meta-learning toward a personalized model:** **pFedMe** (T. Dinh et al., NeurIPS 2020) [4] uses Moreau envelopes to decouple a personalized model from the global one; **Ditto** (Li et al., ICML 2021) [5] trains a per-client model regularized toward the global model and frames personalization explicitly as fairness-plus-robustness; **Per-FedAvg** (Fallah et al., NeurIPS 2020) [6] learns a MAML-style initialization that each client adapts in a few steps. (ii) **Parameter decoupling / partial sharing:** **FedPer** (Arivazhagan et al. 2019) [7] and **FedRep** (Collins et al., ICML 2021) [8] share a global representation but keep client-specific heads; **FedBN** (Li et al., ICLR 2021) [13] keeps **batch-norm layers local** to handle feature shift — a *per-layer* structural choice; **PartialFed** (Sun et al., NeurIPS 2021) [11] learns which parameters to load globally vs. locally. (iii) **Model interpolation / hypernetworks:** **FedFomo** (Zhang et al., ICLR 2021) [9] interpolates each client's model from the most relevant peers via first-order model optimization; **pFedHN** (Shamsian et al., ICML 2021) [10] generates per-client weights from a shared hypernetwork. The feature-**transferability** result of Yosinski et al. (NeurIPS 2014) [21] underpins the parameter-decoupling family: lower layers learn general/transferable features, upper layers learn task-specific ones — which justifies differentiating *per layer* how aggressively a client should personalize.

Orthogonally, adaptive optimizers are used to accelerate and stabilize FL. **FedAdam / FedYogi / FedAdagrad** (Reddi et al., *Adaptive Federated Optimization*, ICLR 2021) [14] apply Adam-style adaptivity to the **server-side** aggregation step, treating the averaged client delta as a pseudo-gradient. Local **Adam** (Kingma & Ba 2015 [23]) is also used inside clients but is known to overfit local noise and amplify drift under Non-IID data. **AdaBound** (Luo et al., *Adaptive Gradient Methods with Dynamic Bound of Learning Rate*, ICLR 2019) [15] clips each per-parameter adaptive learning rate between a **dynamic lower bound and upper bound** that converge to a common value as training proceeds, so the optimizer transitions smoothly Adam→SGD — gaining Adam's early speed and SGD's late stability. Porting this **dynamic bound of learning rate** into local client training is a natural way to stabilize personalization under heterogeneity, and it is the lineage this work extends.

**The gap the rest of this work builds on.** Every such bound schedule is a function of the **global round `t` only** — it is *time-driven, drift-blind, and applied identically to every client and every layer*. Yet heterogeneity, the precise quantity the bound is meant to manage, is **per-client and per-layer and observable at trial time** (via local-vs-global update divergence and local label skew). A round-indexed schedule cannot express that structure: it simultaneously over-regularizes low-drift clients (its converging bound forces a premature Adam→SGD transition that throttles the very personalization pFL exists to deliver) and under-stabilizes high-drift clients (whose local adaptivity overfits label noise and drifts far enough to poison aggregation). Because mean client accuracy marginalizes over drift, the resulting harm is concentrated on — and hidden in — the heterogeneous client tail. This is the opening the present work targets: turn the observable drift signal into a per-client, per-layer modulation of the dynamic bound, with an exact collapse to the AdaBound-FedAvg / FedAvg baseline when drift is zero.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

**(a) The HUSH gap, precisely.** We name the gap **HUSH** — *Heterogeneity-blind, Uniform-Schedule Harm*. Importing AdaBound-style dynamic learning-rate bounds [15] into local FL training carries a real benefit (Adam's early speed, SGD's late stability, damped local-Adam overfitting [23]), but the schedule that carries it is governed by the global round `t` alone and is applied identically to every client and every layer. The control variable — a round index — carries no client identity and no layer identity, while the quantity it is meant to regulate, statistical heterogeneity, is intrinsically per-client and per-layer. The uniform schedule is therefore mis-matched in two opposite directions at once. For a **low-drift client**, whose data are nearly typical, the converging bound forces a premature Adam→SGD transition that *over-regularizes*: it collapses the adaptive window before the client has personalized, suppressing exactly the client-specific adaptation that motivated pFL. For a **high-drift client**, whose data are extremely skewed, the same schedule is too permissive: it leaves the adaptive window open long enough that local Adam *under-stabilizes*, overfitting local label noise and drifting so far from the global anchor that its uploaded update degrades aggregation for everyone. One blind schedule cannot be simultaneously tight enough for the tail and loose enough for the bulk.

**The silence is structural.** The community's headline metric is mean test accuracy averaged over clients, and that average is dominated by the large mass of near-typical, moderate-drift clients. A drift-blind bound can leave the mean flat — or even nudge it upward by helping the typical majority — while the **high-drift tail** (extreme label skew, minority distributions, small or noisy local sets) is silently eroded: per-client accuracy on that tail falls, the count of mis-served clients climbs, and the spread of per-client accuracy widens. Because the standard metric **marginalizes over the client-drift covariate**, none of this registers on the dashboard the operator watches — the same metric-blindness pathology that recurs whenever a population metric is optimized over a structured subpopulation harm. HUSH names this uncorrected, average-accuracy-invisible erosion, with the sharp falsifiable property that it **vanishes if and only if** the per-client per-layer drift signal is identically zero (the IID case), where there is nothing to differentiate.

**(b) TETHER, the corrective.** We introduce **TETHER**, which replaces the time-only bound with a **per-client `k`, per-layer `ℓ`, drift-conditioned** bound gap. The base AdaBound width `g_base(t)` (the converging Adam→SGD window) is modulated by an **observable local heterogeneity signal** `d_{k,ℓ}`: the normalized divergence between client `k`'s accumulated local update on layer `ℓ` and the freshly-downloaded global step direction (cosine/`ℓ2` form), optionally fused with the client's local **label-distribution skew** — a quantity that needs no extra communication and exposes no raw data. The modulated gap is

  `g_{k,ℓ}(t) = g_base(t) · m(d_{k,ℓ})`,  with `m: ℝ_{≥0} → (0,1]` monotone non-increasing and `m(0)=1`.

The logic is **tighten-for-drift, loosen-for-typicality**: a high-drift layer of a high-drift client gets a narrow window (fast Adam→SGD, *tethered* to the global model, suppressing overfit and keeping its update aggregable), while a low-drift layer keeps the full base window (free to personalize). The differentiation is **per-layer** and grounded in feature transferability (Yosinski et al. 2014 [21]): lower, transferable layers are tethered tighter (kept anchored to the shared representation), upper, task-specific layers are freed sooner. TETHER acts **purely inside the local optimizer's LR-clipping step** — no change to the aggregation rule, no server state beyond the already-broadcast global model, and (unlike SCAFFOLD's control variates [3]) no extra communication and no extra uploaded variables.

**(c) The EXACT-COLLAPSE property.** The modulation is defined so that `d_{k,ℓ} → 0 ⇒ m(d_{k,ℓ}) → m(0) = 1 ⇒ g_{k,ℓ}(t) → g_base(t)` identically for every client and layer. Two regimes must be distinguished. In **modulation-off / forced-`d=0` (shadow) mode** — the deployable safe-rollout path — `m` is clamped to 1, so the bound gap is *exactly* `g_base(t)` and TETHER reproduces the AdaBound-FedAvg optimizer to floating-point tolerance (`E_collapse ≤ 1e-6`); here "step-for-step / decision-for-decision" means *up to that float tolerance*, not literal bit equality. With the **live signal active on a finite IID sample**, the label-skew component `s_k` is exactly zero, but the update-divergence component `d^div` is zero only *in expectation* under finite-sample SGD — realized per-client divergence is small but nonzero — so collapse holds up to SGD sampling noise, which the same `1e-6` tolerance gate absorbs. As the base bounds themselves converge, the optimizer further approaches (Adam/SGD-)FedAvg [1]. This makes TETHER a **strict, safe generalization** rather than a risky rewrite: an operator can ship it in *shadow mode*, machine-verify reproduction of the deployed baseline within tolerance at zero drift, and only then enable modulation on the high-drift clients, with a guarantee of zero regression (within tolerance) wherever there is no heterogeneity to correct. Turning TETHER on can only **add** corrections on the tail the baseline already misses.

**(d) The novelty wedge (analytical punchline).** TETHER's contribution is the *conjunction* of four clauses, and no prior method covers more than two of them at once: **C1** — local-optimizer only, zero communication overhead (no extra rounds, no uploaded variables, no server state beyond the global model), in contrast even to explicit communication-efficiency methods such as FedPAQ [22] that *compress or quantize* the uploaded model rather than leaving the communication footprint untouched; **C2** — the bound is conditioned on an *observable per-client, per-layer drift signal* `d_{k,ℓ}`, not on a round index; **C3** — *per-layer* bound differentiation grounded in feature transferability [21], a continuous signal-driven split rather than a fixed structural one; **C4** — a provable *exact collapse* to AdaBound-FedAvg / FedAvg at the IID default (`E_collapse ≤ 1e-6`). The dynamic-bound idea lived in single-machine optimization as a time-only clock [15]; drift-correction lived on the aggregation side as control variates or a uniform proximal scalar [2,3]; the per-layer idea lived in parameter-decoupling pFL as a static structural split [7,8,13]. TETHER is the first to fuse an observable, per-client, per-layer drift measurement *into the local dynamic LR bound* and attach a safety collapse that makes doing so deployable.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can replacing personalized federated learning's time-uniform, drift-blind learning-rate-bound schedule with a per-client, per-layer dynamic bound whose tightness is driven by an observable local-versus-global update-divergence signal reduce accuracy loss on the high-drift tail of clients while provably collapsing, step-for-step, to the AdaBound-FedAvg / FedAvg optimizer at the IID zero-drift default with zero mean-accuracy regression?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

**P1 — A latent weakness in the dominant heterogeneity-management pattern, not a corner case.** Statistical heterogeneity — Non-IID, unbalanced client data — is not one obstacle among many in federated learning; it is *the* central, defining problem the field organizes itself around (Kairouz et al. 2021 [17]; Zhao et al. 2018 [18]), the reason FedProx [2], SCAFFOLD [3], FedNova [12] and the entire personalized-FL program [16] exist. Within that program, importing AdaBound-style *dynamic learning-rate bounds* (Luo et al. 2019 [15]) into local client training is a genuinely promising stabilizer: it gives each client Adam's early speed and SGD's late stability while damping the local-Adam overfitting [23] that Non-IID data is known to induce. But the bound schedule that carries this benefit is a function of the **global round `t` only** — it is time-driven, drift-blind, and applied **identically to every client and every layer**. That uniformity is a *structural* property of the whole pFL+adaptive-optimization pattern, not a hyperparameter that a practitioner forgot to tune: the very quantity the bound is meant to manage (heterogeneity) is per-client and per-layer, yet the control variable (a round index) carries no client or layer identity. The schedule therefore simultaneously **over-constrains low-drift clients** — its converging bound forces a premature Adam→SGD transition that throttles the personalization pFL was deployed to deliver — and **under-stabilizes high-drift clients**, whose local adaptivity overfits label noise and drifts far enough to poison aggregation. A weakness that is built into the dominant pattern and scales with the central problem of the field is a broad latent vulnerability, which is precisely what makes closing it valuable rather than incremental.

**P2 — The failure is SILENT under mean/global accuracy (the HUSH property).** The FL community's headline metric is mean test accuracy averaged over clients, and that average is dominated by the large mass of near-typical, moderate-drift clients. A drift-blind bound can leave the average flat — or even nudge it upward by helping the typical majority — while the **high-drift tail** (extreme label skew, minority distributions, small or noisy local sets) is silently eroded: per-client accuracy on that tail falls, the count of mis-served / locked-out clients climbs, and the spread of per-client accuracy widens. Because the standard metric **marginalizes over the client-drift covariate**, none of this registers on the dashboard the operator actually watches. This is the same metric-blindness pathology that recurs whenever a population metric is optimized over a structured subpopulation harm — an operator optimizing the wrong aggregate concludes nothing is wrong while a specific subpopulation is progressively excluded. HUSH (Heterogeneity-blind, Uniform-Schedule Harm) names exactly this uncorrected, average-accuracy-invisible erosion concentrated on the heterogeneous tail, with the sharp falsifiable property that it **vanishes if and only if** the per-client per-layer drift signal is identically zero (the IID case), where there is nothing to differentiate. A harm you cannot see on your dashboard is one you will never fix unaided; making it visible is half the contribution.

**P3 — Client-level fairness and disparate impact along the heterogeneity axis.** The clients HUSH harms are not a random sample — they are systematically the heterogeneous tail: minority data distributions, underrepresented devices, regions, and languages, and small or skewed local sets. These are frequently the participants federated learning is most often justified as *protecting* (data stays on-device precisely for under-resourced or privacy-sensitive populations), so degrading them while the mean rises is a textbook disparate-impact pattern that the headline metric is structurally unable to expose. The pFL literature has already established that minimizing average loss is an inadequate objective and that per-client outcomes must be governed directly: Ditto [5] frames personalization explicitly as a fairness-and-robustness problem and reports per-client accuracy dispersion as a first-class quantity; q-FFL [24] reweights the federated objective to equalize accuracy across clients; agnostic FL (Mohri et al. 2019 [27]) optimizes the worst-case client mixture rather than the average. Against this backdrop, **widening the per-client accuracy spread** — exactly what a drift-blind bound does to the tail — is a recognized fairness failure, not a cosmetic one, and it is invisible to the very metric most FL papers report. TETHER's corrections land where the fairness literature says they must: on the worst-off, highest-drift clients.

**P4 — Deployability via EXACT COLLAPSE: a strict, safe generalization, not a risky rewrite.** TETHER is a drop-in change to the **local optimizer's LR-clipping step**: it adds no communication rounds, uploads no extra variables (unlike SCAFFOLD's control variates [3]), and requires no change to the server aggregation rule or any server state beyond the global model already broadcast. Critically, the modulation is defined so that when the observable drift signal `d_{k,ℓ}→0`, the modulation factor →1 and the bound gap reverts to the **base AdaBound schedule identically for every client and layer** — so in modulation-off / forced-`d=0` shadow mode TETHER reproduces the AdaBound-FedAvg optimizer to floating-point tolerance, and as the base bounds themselves converge, further approaches (Adam/SGD-)FedAvg [1]. This exact collapse (`E_collapse ≤ 1e-6`, a floating-point-tolerance CI build gate — "decision-for-decision" meaning *up to that tolerance*, with the live finite-IID-sample case holding up to residual SGD noise the same gate absorbs) is the deployability unlock, not a footnote: an operator can ship TETHER in **shadow mode**, machine-verify within-tolerance reproduction of the deployed baseline at zero drift, and only then enable modulation on the high-drift clients — with a guarantee that there is zero regression (within tolerance) wherever there is no heterogeneity to correct. Turning TETHER on can only **add** corrections on the tail the baseline already misses. This converts the idea from an analysis into an adoptable component with a quantified safety contract.

**P5 — Generality: a control-signal recipe, not a one-off optimizer tweak.** The core move — convert an *observable, per-client, per-layer* heterogeneity statistic into a **control signal that modulates an existing hyperparameter**, rather than fixing that hyperparameter to a blind schedule — is general. The same local-vs-global update-divergence and label-skew signal that tightens TETHER's LR bound could equally modulate **FedProx's proximal coefficient μ** [2] (anchor drifting clients harder, free typical ones), the **server learning rate in FedAdam** [14] (down-weight rounds dominated by high-drift pseudo-gradients), or the **staleness weight in asynchronous FL**. In each case the recipe is identical: replace a fixed or time-indexed schedule with a measured-heterogeneity control law that exact-collapses to the schedule when drift is zero. TETHER is thus a concrete, fully worked instance of a broadly reusable principle — *operationalize the measured heterogeneity, do not assume it away with a clock* — which raises the contribution's reach well beyond the single optimizer it is first demonstrated on.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

TETHER must drive an AdaBound-style learning-rate bound with a *per-client, per-layer drift signal* that is generated by the very optimization it controls, measured under sampling noise, and evaluated against a metric that is structurally blind to the population it is meant to protect. Four obstacles follow; **O1 is BINDING** — it forces TETHER's core design (a causal/lagged, warm-start drift estimator plus a monotone, contractive modulation `m` with `m(0)=1`). The others (O2–O4) shape the parameterization, the signal normalization, and the evaluation, but O1 is the one that makes the naive design *structurally* impossible rather than merely suboptimal.

**O1 (BINDING) — ENDOGENEITY / FEEDBACK OF THE CONTROL SIGNAL.** The drift signal `d_{k,ℓ}` is not an exogenous covariate read off the input (unlike, say, an age gap or a timestamp): it is *produced by* the local optimization that the bound is supposed to regulate. This creates two coupled failures that a "measure-after, apply-now" design cannot escape. (1) **Temporal unavailability.** The full local-vs-global divergence — the natural definition of `d_{k,ℓ}` — is only known *after* the client finishes its local epochs, but the bound gap `g_{k,ℓ}(t)` must be set *before* (or during) those same epochs. You cannot clip a learning rate with a number you will not have until training is over; the most informative estimate is causally posterior to the decision it must inform. (2) **Closed-loop instability.** Suppose one naively closes the loop within a round (re-measure divergence every few steps and re-tighten). The control law is then self-defeating: tightening the bound for a high-drift client *reduces* its subsequent drift, which makes the next-step signal report *low* drift, which *loosens* the bound, which *increases* drift again — a limit cycle. The bound oscillates between Adam-like and SGD-like regimes within a single client's local training, and the per-parameter clip never settles, degrading convergence precisely on the tail clients TETHER exists to help. This is a **representational, not a tuning** obstacle: no learning-rate or smoothing constant rescues a controller whose feedback sign and timing are wrong by construction. It forces two non-negotiable design commitments. First, a **causal/lagged drift estimator**: either the *previous round's* completed divergence `d_{k,ℓ}^{(t-1)}` (available before round `t` begins, an exogenous-at-decision-time proxy), or a **warm-start divergence** measured in the first few local steps on the *freshly downloaded global anchor* `w^t` before the bound is engaged — both break the within-round feedback by decoupling the signal's measurement from the action it triggers this round. Second, a **monotone, contractive modulation** `m`: `m` must be monotone non-increasing in `d` (more drift ⇒ tighter gap), be built to satisfy a contraction property (`m` composed with the local-update map is designed with a spectral/Lipschitz factor `< 1` for bounded `λ`, so any residual feedback is *damped* toward a fixed point rather than amplified into oscillation), and satisfy `m(0)=1` for exact collapse. The contraction is a **design commitment guaranteed by the construction of `m` with bounded `λ`, demonstrated empirically via ablation 5, not a proven convergence theorem**. The unconstrained alternative — a free, possibly non-monotone modulation re-fit per step — is exactly the design we predict will oscillate, and we ablate it (below) to *demonstrate* O1 empirically rather than merely assert it.

**O2 — GRANULARITY vs. IDENTIFIABILITY.** A genuinely "per-client × per-layer" bound is a large, high-variance parameter set: with `K` clients and `L` layers, freely fitting lower/upper clips per `(k,ℓ,parameter)` is wildly over-parameterized and will overfit local noise, defeating the stabilization goal. A naive "give every layer of every client its own free bounds" design has more knobs than the per-round client data can identify, so the bounds chase sampling artifacts. This forces a **low-dimensional parameterization**: TETHER does *not* learn free per-parameter bounds; it keeps the single base AdaBound schedule `g_base(t)` and applies *one scalar modulation per layer per client*, `m(d_{k,ℓ}) ∈ (0,1]`, driven entirely by the observable signal. The degrees of freedom collapse from `O(K·L·P)` free clips to a single fixed-form function `m` shared across all clients (hierarchical sharing: the *function* is global, only its *input* `d_{k,ℓ}` is local), which is identifiable from a single round's drift measurement and cannot overfit because it has no per-client free parameters to fit.

**O3 — CONFOUNDING SAMPLING NOISE vs. GENUINE DRIFT.** A small or noisy client produces a high-variance local update that *looks* far from the global anchor — large `d_{k,ℓ}` — even when its underlying label/feature distribution is perfectly typical (it is just under-sampled). A divergence-only signal therefore mistakes *estimation noise* for *distributional heterogeneity* and tightens the bound on clients that do not need anchoring, throttling their personalization for no reason. The naive "raw cosine/ℓ2 distance" design conflates the two. This forces the signal to be **normalized by gradient/update variance** (e.g., divide the divergence by an estimate of the local update's stochastic variance, or use a SNR-style normalized divergence so that noise-driven distance is discounted) and/or **fused with a sampling-independent label-skew signal** (a normalized entropy or ℓ1 distance of the local label histogram to uniform), which is a *direct* read on distributional heterogeneity that does not inflate under small-sample noise. The fused signal tracks genuine drift, not the number of local examples.

**O4 — METRIC BLINDNESS.** The community-standard headline — mean/global client accuracy — *cannot see* either the HUSH harm or the TETHER fix, because it marginalizes over the client-drift covariate: the high-drift tail is a small minority of clients whose degradation (or recovery) is averaged away by the mass of typical clients. A design validated on mean accuracy alone is therefore unfalsifiable — the very effect it targets is invisible in its primary number. This forces **drift-stratified, worst-k% evaluation as first-class metrics**: clients are binned by their measured drift, accuracy is reported *per drift bin* and on the *worst-10% tail*, per-client accuracy spread/variance is tracked, and a client-fairness gap is reported alongside the mean. Without this evaluation apparatus the contribution is literally unmeasurable; with it, the hypothesis ("gains concentrate in high-drift bins, vanish at zero drift, mean unchanged") becomes testable and refutable.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

TETHER's contribution is the *conjunction* of four clauses; the wedge is that **no prior method covers more than two of them at once.**

- **C1 — Local-optimizer only, zero communication overhead.** TETHER acts purely inside the per-parameter LR-clipping step of local training. It adds **no communication rounds, no extra uploaded variables, and no server-side state beyond the already-broadcast global model** — explicitly unlike SCAFFOLD's control variates [3], which double per-round communication to correct drift on the aggregation side.
- **C2 — Bound conditioned on an OBSERVABLE per-client, per-layer drift signal.** The bound tightness is a function of a measured local heterogeneity quantity — the normalized divergence between the client's accumulated local update and the freshly-downloaded global step direction (cosine/ℓ2), optionally fused with local label-skew — **not** of a round index. This is the precise inversion of AdaBound's time-only schedule [15]: the control variable now carries client and layer identity and is computable at trial time without exposing raw data.
- **C3 — Per-layer bound differentiation grounded in feature transferability.** Lower, transferable layers are tethered tighter (kept anchored to the global model) and upper, task-specific layers are freed sooner, grounded in the transferability result of Yosinski et al. 2014 [21]. This is a *signal-driven, continuous* per-layer differentiation, not a single per-model bound and not a fixed structural split such as FedBN's batch-norm-only locality [13] or FedPer/FedRep's frozen head/body partition [7,8].
- **C4 — Provable EXACT collapse to AdaBound-FedAvg / FedAvg at the IID default.** When drift is identically zero the modulation is identically 1 and TETHER reproduces the deployed baseline **step-for-step**, a strict safe generalization with `E_collapse ≤ 1e-6` enforced as a CI gate. Turning it on can only add tail corrections; it can never regress the zero-drift case.

*Near-miss dissection — every named prior covers at most two clauses.*

- **AdaBound [15] — the collapse target (covers a weakened C2 only).** AdaBound originates the *dynamic* lower/upper bound on the per-parameter LR, so it is C2-adjacent — but its bound is a function of **time (the global step count) alone**, with no notion of a client, a layer, or an observable heterogeneity signal. In FL it is applied identically everywhere. It misses C1-in-FL (it has no FL deployment story), C3 (single per-model bound), and C4 (it *is* the baseline TETHER collapses *to*, so it cannot itself supply the collapse guarantee). Covers ≈1 of 4. **This is the closest near-miss because TETHER is a strict super-set of it.**
- **FedProx [2] — drift-anchoring without a bound (covers neither C2 nor C3 properly).** FedProx anchors local training to the global model with a proximal term `(μ/2)‖w−w^t‖²`, conceptually adjacent to "tether drifting clients." But μ is a **single uniform hyperparameter**: it is not a learning-rate bound, it is not per-layer, and it is **not conditioned on any observable drift signal** — every client uses the same μ regardless of its measured heterogeneity. It satisfies C1 (local-only) and nothing else of substance. Covers 1 of 4.
- **SCAFFOLD [3] — drift correction on the wrong side of the wire (fails C1).** SCAFFOLD directly measures and corrects client-drift via server/client control variates, so it engages the drift phenomenon — but it does so by **doubling per-round communication** and adding uploaded state, violating C1. It corrects the update *direction*, not an LR *bound* (no C2-as-a-bound), is uniform across layers (no C3), and offers no exact-collapse safety contract (no C4). Covers ≈1 of 4.
- **FedBN [13] — per-layer but structurally fixed and drift-blind (covers C1 + a frozen C3).** FedBN keeps batch-norm layers local to handle feature shift, a genuine *per-layer* structural choice (C1, partial C3). But the split is **fixed and BN-only**: it is not driven by any observable drift signal, it imposes no learning-rate bound, and it has no zero-drift collapse guarantee. Its per-layer treatment is a static partition, not a continuous, measured modulation. Covers ≈2 of 4 (C1 + a rigid C3), and is therefore a near-miss on the per-layer clause specifically — but it misses C2 and C4 entirely.
- **FedAdam / FedYogi / FedAdagrad [14] — adaptivity on the server, not the client (fails C2's locus).** Reddi et al. apply Adam-style adaptivity to the **server-side** aggregation step, treating the mean client delta as a pseudo-gradient. This is adaptive, but it is **not per-client** (it acts after averaging, so it has already marginalized over drift — the very HUSH pathology), not per-layer in the drift sense, and imposes **no dynamic LR bound** at all. It satisfies neither C2 (no per-client observable drift conditioning) nor C3 nor C4. Covers ≈1 of 4.
- **pFedMe [4] / Ditto [5] / Per-FedAvg [6] / FedFomo [9] / pFedHN [10] — different personalization mechanisms entirely.** These personalize through Moreau-envelope decoupling [4], proximal per-client models with a fairness framing [5], MAML-style adaptable initializations [6], peer model interpolation [9], or hypernetwork-generated weights [10]. Each may satisfy C1 (most are local/aggregation-side without extra comms), and Ditto touches the fairness motivation behind P3 — but **none conditions a learning-rate bound on an observable per-layer drift signal, and none provides an exact step-for-step collapse to AdaBound-FedAvg/FedAvg.** They personalize the *model*; TETHER conditions the *optimizer's bound*. Each covers at most ≈1 of 4 of the specific wedge.

*The barrier — why the fusion was never made.* The reason no one assembled C1–C4 is a **conceptual division of labor**, not a missing piece of math. Two communities each held one half of the answer and neither crossed the boundary. The **optimization** community that introduced the dynamic LR bound treated it as a *global optimization schedule* — a smooth Adam→SGD transition governed by training progress (a clock), inheriting the single-machine framing where there is no "client" and no "drift" to condition on; porting it to FL preserved that time-only, uniform schedule unquestioned. The **heterogeneity / drift-correction** community (SCAFFOLD [3], FedProx [2], FedNova [12]) treated client drift as an **aggregation-side problem** — something to measure and subtract at the server, or to anchor with a uniform scalar — never as a signal that should reach *into the local optimizer's learning-rate bound*. Meanwhile the per-layer insight lived in a third place (parameter-decoupling pFL [7,8,13]) as a *static structural* split, divorced from both the bound and the drift signal. **Nobody fused an observable, per-client, per-layer drift measurement INTO the local dynamic LR bound, and nobody attached a safety collapse to make doing so deployable.** Each ingredient existed; the specific composition — drift-conditioned, per-layer, local-optimizer bound modulation with an exact zero-drift collapse — fell in the seam between the three subfields, which is exactly why it is both novel and overdue.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

*Q5a — Method formalization.* **Base local optimizer (the collapse target):** each client runs local AdaBound [15] — a per-parameter adaptive step `η̂_t` (Adam-style) clipped into a window whose lower and upper bounds *converge* with the round/step index, `Clip(η̂_t, η_l(t), η_u(t))`, with `η_l(t) ↗ η*` and `η_u(t) ↘ η*` so that `η_u(t) − η_l(t) → 0` and the optimizer transitions Adam→SGD. Define the **base bound gap** `g_base(t) = η_u(t) − η_l(t)` (the standard AdaBound width), centered on the running `η*`. **TETHER modulation:** replace the time-only gap with a per-client `k`, per-layer `ℓ` gap `g_{k,ℓ}(t) = g_base(t) · m(d_{k,ℓ})`, where `m: ℝ_{≥0} → (0,1]` is **monotone non-increasing** and **contractive**, with `m(0)=1`. The window stays centered on `η*` and is *narrowed* toward the SGD anchor as drift rises: `η_l^{k,ℓ}(t) = η* − ½ g_{k,ℓ}(t)`, `η_u^{k,ℓ}(t) = η* + ½ g_{k,ℓ}(t)`. A high-drift layer of a high-drift client gets a tight window (fast Adam→SGD, anchored to global); a low-drift layer gets the full base window (free to personalize). A concrete admissible `m` is `m(d) = exp(−λ·d̃)` or `m(d) = 1/(1+λ·d̃)` with `λ ≥ 0` a single global temperature; both are monotone, satisfy `m(0)=1`, and are Lipschitz-contractive for bounded `λ`. **Drift signal `d_{k,ℓ}` (lagged / warm-start, per O1):** let `Δ_{k,ℓ}` be client `k`'s local update on layer `ℓ` and `Δ^{global}_ℓ` the global step direction on layer `ℓ`; define `d^{div}_{k,ℓ} = 1 − cos(Δ_{k,ℓ}, Δ^{global}_ℓ)` (cosine) or `‖Δ_{k,ℓ} − Δ^{global}_ℓ‖₂ / (‖Δ^{global}_ℓ‖₂ + ε)` (ℓ2). To satisfy O1, `Δ_{k,ℓ}` is taken from the **previous round** (`d_{k,ℓ}^{(t-1)}`) or from a **warm-start probe** (the divergence of the first few local steps on `w^t_ℓ`, before the bound is engaged). To satisfy O3, normalize by the local update variance, `d̃^{div}_{k,ℓ} = d^{div}_{k,ℓ} / (1 + σ̂²_{k,ℓ})`, and optionally **fuse with label-skew** `s_k = ‖p_k − u‖₁` or `1 − H(p_k)/log C`, giving `d̃_{k,ℓ} = (1−β)·d̃^{div}_{k,ℓ} + β·s_k`; label skew needs **no extra communication** and exposes no raw data. **Exact-collapse lemma:** *if `d_{k,ℓ} = 0` for all `k,ℓ` then for every step the TETHER update equals the AdaBound-FedAvg update to floating-point tolerance.* Proof sketch: `d_{k,ℓ}=0 ⇒ m(0)=1 ⇒ g_{k,ℓ}(t)=g_base(t) ⇒` the clip window is identical to base AdaBound for every client/layer; aggregation is unchanged (FedAvg); hence the per-parameter clipped step, the local trajectory, and the aggregated global model coincide, and as `g_base→0` the optimizer further approaches (Adam/SGD-)FedAvg. The `d=0` precondition is met *exactly* in modulation-off / forced-`d=0` shadow mode (the deployable safe-rollout path); with the live signal on a finite IID sample the label-skew term `s_k` is exactly zero while the divergence term `d^div` is zero only *in expectation* under finite-sample SGD, so realized collapse holds up to SGD noise absorbed by the tolerance gate. **CI gate:** `E_collapse = max over steps of ‖θ_TETHER − θ_{AdaBound-FedAvg}‖_∞ ≤ 1e-6` (a floating-point tolerance, not literal bit equality), enforced as a build-failing test on an IID partition in shadow mode — with no extra uploaded variables, no extra communication, and no server state beyond the global model (contrast SCAFFOLD [3]).

*Q5b — Datasets / benchmarks.* **CIFAR-10** and **CIFAR-100** with **Dirichlet Non-IID partitions**, concentration `α ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5}` [26] (lower `α` ⇒ heavier label skew ⇒ thicker high-drift tail; `α→∞` gives the near-IID collapse check); **FEMNIST** (per-writer Non-IID) and **Shakespeare** (per-speaker next-character) from **LEAF** [25]; optionally **Tiny-ImageNet** for scale. **Models:** a standard CNN (CIFAR/FEMNIST), **ResNet-18** (CIFAR/Tiny-ImageNet) — with the **FedBN caveat** [13] that batch-norm statistics under feature shift should be kept local or replaced by GroupNorm so BN drift does not confound the layer-wise signal — and a **2-layer LSTM** for Shakespeare. **Scale/compute:** standard FL simulation (Flower or FedML), **100–500 clients** with per-round client sampling, a few GPUs; no extra wall-clock cost vs. FedAvg since TETHER is local-only.

*Q5c — Baselines.* FedAvg [1]; FedProx [2]; SCAFFOLD [3]; FedAdam [14]; **AdaBound-FedAvg** (the naive time-only port = the exact-collapse target [15]); pFedMe [4]; Ditto [5]; FedBN [13]. AdaBound-FedAvg is the *primary* comparison: TETHER must equal it at IID and beat it on the high-drift tail.

*Q5d — Ablations.* (1) **Time-only vs. drift-conditioned** bound (`m≡1` vs. `m(d_{k,ℓ})`) — isolates the core contribution; (2) **per-model vs. per-layer** modulation — tests the transferability-grounded per-layer claim; (3) **divergence-only vs. divergence+label-skew** — tests the O3 fusion; (4) **lagged vs. within-round (warm-start)** drift estimate — tests the O1 causal-estimator choice; (5) **monotone-contractive vs. unconstrained** modulation — **the O1 demonstration**: the unconstrained variant is predicted to oscillate (non-converging within-client bound), empirically validating that O1 forces the contractive design.

*Q5e — Metrics (tail-stratified, first-class).* **Mean client accuracy** *with an equivalence/regression check* (TOST-style two-one-sided test) at IID/high-`α` — must show *no regression*, not merely "not worse on average"; **worst-10% client accuracy** (primary tail metric); **per-client accuracy variance / spread**; **accuracy on the high-drift subset, stratified by drift bin** (the effect must localize to high-drift bins); **client-fairness gap** (max−min or top−bottom-decile client accuracy; q-FFL-style [24]); **communication cost** — must show **parity with FedAvg**; and **`E_collapse ≤ 1e-6`** on the IID partition (hard CI gate). Mean accuracy marginalizes over drift, so it can see *neither* the HUSH harm *nor* the TETHER fix; only drift-stratified, worst-k% metrics make the contribution measurable and falsifiable (O4).

*Q5f — Expected outcomes (falsifiable hypotheses).* **H1 (tail gain, localized):** worst-10% and high-drift-bin accuracy improve over AdaBound-FedAvg, with the gain *monotonically increasing in drift* and **tapering to 0 as drift→0** (lowest-`α` bins gain most; near-IID gains ≈ 0). **H2 (no mean regression):** mean accuracy is *unchanged within an equivalence CI* (TOST passes) at IID/high-`α`. **H3 (exact collapse):** `E_collapse ≤ 1e-6` in forced-`d=0` shadow mode (a floating-point-tolerance CI gate); with the live signal on a finite IID sample, collapse holds within the same tolerance up to residual SGD noise. **H4 (ablations):** **per-layer > per-model** modulation; **divergence+label-skew > divergence-only** on noisy/small clients; **lagged/warm-start** matches or beats within-round; and the **unconstrained modulation oscillates** (non-converging within-client bound, degraded tail accuracy) — directly validating O1. **H5 (parity):** communication cost equals FedAvg; per-round compute overhead is a small constant (one divergence dot-product per layer). Every claimed contribution maps to an experiment that can confirm or refute it: the collapse to the `E_collapse` gate; the tail benefit to the drift-stratified worst-10% metric; the per-layer and causal-estimator claims to ablations 2 and 4; and the binding obstacle O1 to ablation 5's oscillation result.

*Q5g — Responsible-AI and deployment considerations.* **Privacy.** TETHER's drift and label-skew signals are computed and consumed **strictly on-device** and are **never transmitted or logged server-side**: a per-client label histogram would directly leak the client's class distribution, so it must remain local; if any such signal were ever shared for telemetry it must first be differentially-privately noised. The signal therefore adds no communication and exposes no raw on-device data, consistent with FL's data-minimization premise. **Fairness residual (explicit guard).** Conditioning bound tightness on label skew carries a residual risk: a legitimately heterogeneous minority client could be **over-tethered** — its window narrowed toward the SGD anchor — throttling the very personalization it most needs. Four guards bound this risk: (i) the O3 variance-normalization, together with the update-divergence signal, distinguishes genuine distributional drift from small-sample noise so atypical-but-valid clients are not penalized as noise; (ii) the label-skew fusion weight `β` can be reduced or set to 0 so a client is never penalized for skew alone; (iii) the worst-k% and per-drift-bin metrics surface any such regression as a first-class, reported result rather than hiding it in the mean; and (iv) TETHER only **narrows** the adaptive window toward the SGD anchor — it never blocks personalization, and at the identity default (`m=1`) it collapses to the baseline. **Disparate impact** is surfaced rather than averaged away by reporting a full per-drift-bin and worst-decile client-accuracy table as a primary metric [5,24,27]. **Safe rollout.** The exact-collapse contract (`E_collapse ≤ 1e-6` in shadow mode) is the rollout mechanism: TETHER is first enabled in shadow mode, machine-verified to reproduce the deployed AdaBound-FedAvg baseline within tolerance at zero drift, and only then dialed up on high-drift clients, so no client is regressed where there is no heterogeneity to correct.
